content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 24 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 112v–112r | **Source Images:** DEU_024_page_001.jpg, DEU_024_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 157–158 | **LXX Images:** DEU_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** When a man takes a wife and marries her, and it comes about that she finds no favor in his eyes because he has found in her an indecent thing [ervat davar], and he writes for her a scroll of cutting off [sefer keritut] and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house—

**2** and she goes out of his house and goes and becomes another man's wife—

**3** and the latter man hates her and writes for her a scroll of cutting off and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house, or if the latter man who took her as his wife dies—

**4** then her former husband who sent her away is not permitted to take her again as his wife after she has been made impure; for that is an abomination before the LORD, and you shall not bring sin upon the land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

**5** When a man is newly taken as a husband, he shall not go out with the army, and he shall not be charged with any duty; he shall be free at home for one year and shall gladden his wife whom he has taken.

**6** No one shall take a mill or an upper millstone as a pledge; for he would be taking a life as a pledge.

**7** If a man is found stealing a person [nefesh] from his brothers, from the sons of Yisra'el, and he treats him as a slave and sells him, then that thief shall die; so you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**8** Be watchful with regard to the skin disease [nega tsara'at], to be very careful to do according to all that the Levitical priests teach you; as I have commanded them, so you shall be careful to do.

**9** Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the way when you came out of Egypt.

**10** When you lend your neighbor any [kind of] loan, you shall not go into his house to take his pledge.

**11** Outside you shall stand, and the man to whom you are lending shall bring the pledge out to you.

**12** And if he is a poor man, you shall not sleep with his pledge;

**13** you shall certainly return the pledge to him at the setting of the sun, that he may sleep in his garment and bless you; and it shall be righteousness [tsedaqah] to you before the LORD your God.

**14** You shall not oppress a hired servant who is poor and needy, whether [he is] from your brothers or from your sojourner who is in your land within your gates.

**15** In his day you shall give him his wages, and the sun shall not set upon it, for he is poor, and toward it he lifts his soul; lest he cry against you to the LORD, and it be sin in you.

**16** Fathers shall not be put to death on account of sons, and sons shall not be put to death on account of fathers; each man shall be put to death for his own sin.

**17** You shall not pervert the justice due to the sojourner [ger] or to the orphan, and you shall not take as pledge the garment of a widow.

**18** But you shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you from there; therefore I am commanding you to do this thing.

**19** When you reap your harvest in your field and forget a sheaf in the field, you shall not go back to take it; it shall be for the sojourner, for the orphan, and for the widow, so that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hands.

**20** When you beat your olive tree, you shall not go over the boughs again; it shall be for the sojourner, for the orphan, and for the widow.

**21** When you harvest your vineyard, you shall not glean after yourself; it shall be for the sojourner, for the orphan, and for the widow.

**22** And you shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt; therefore I am commanding you to do this thing.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 "indecent thing [ervat davar]"** — Hebrew *ervat davar* (lit. "nakedness of a thing/matter"). This is the same phrase used at 23:14 in the camp purity laws. In the divorce context it generated the famous dispute between the School of Shammai (who limited it to sexual infidelity) and the School of Hillel (who extended it to any displeasure). EMET renders literally without adjudicating this dispute.

**v.1 "scroll of cutting off [sefer keritut]"** — Hebrew *sefer keritut*, lit. "document of cutting." The root *karat* means "to cut off/sever." This is the technical term for the bill of divorce (*get* in later rabbinic tradition). EMET renders literally to expose the root metaphor of legal severance.

**v.1–4 Syntactic structure** — Critical note: the entire divorce law (vv.1–4) is a single conditional sentence in MT: "When a man takes a wife... [vv.1–3]... then her former husband... [v.4]." The law does not prescribe or commend divorce; it regulates what happens when divorce and remarriage have already occurred. EMET preserves this conditional structure exactly. Many translations obscure it by making v.1 a prescriptive statement.

**v.5 "newly taken as a husband"** — Hebrew *ki yiqqach ish ishah chadashah*, lit. "when a man takes a new wife." The MT reads "new wife" rather than "newly married man"—EMET renders "newly taken as a husband" following the syntactic logic; the newness attaches to the status of husband. The LXX reads *neogynaikos* ("newly-wived"), also somewhat ambiguous.

**v.6 "taking a life as a pledge"** — Hebrew *ki nefesh hu choveil*, lit. "for it is a soul/life that he pledges." EMET renders "taking a life as a pledge" to expose the metaphor: the millstone grinds grain to sustain life; pledging it is tantamount to pledging the person's very life.

**v.7 "stealing a person [nefesh]"** — Hebrew *gonev nefesh*, lit. "stealing a soul/person." This is the kidnapping law; the MT term *nefesh* ("soul/person") underscores that a human being is the object of theft. EMET renders "person [nefesh]" with the bracketed Hebrew.

**v.13 "tsedaqah"** — Hebrew *tsedaqah* (righteousness/justice). Returning the poor man's pledge at sunset is credited to the lender as *tsedaqah*. EMET renders "righteousness [tsedaqah]" and preserves the transliteration.

**v.16 Individual responsibility** — This verse establishes the principle of individual accountability (*avot lo yamuthu al banim...ish be-cheto yamuthu*). It stands in explicit contrast to the earlier generational curse formulae. The LXX (Vaticanus p. 158) renders *hekastos en te heautou hamartia apothanountai* ("each shall die by his own sin"), closely matching MT.

**v.19–21 Triple gleaning formula** — Each of the three agricultural gleaning laws (sheaf, olive, vineyard) ends with the identical beneficiary triplet: *la-ger la-yatom ve-la-almanah* ("for the sojourner, for the orphan, and for the widow"). EMET reproduces this repetition exactly in all three verses. Vaticanus p. 158 also preserves all three instances of the triplet without condensation.

**LXX comparison, vv.1–4** — Vaticanus p. 157 (lower portion) treats the divorce pericope as a conditional structure consistent with MT, reinforcing the reading that vv.1–4 is a single protasis-apodosis unit. No major additions or omissions noted throughout the chapter.

*— EMET*
